statement.en: Navigating the Trilingual Policy: Implementation Realities and Pedagogical Tensions in Cameroonian Primary Classrooms
statement.summary: Cameroon's trilingual education policy mandates the use of English, French, and a national language as mediums of instruction in primary schools. This ambitious policy aims to foster national integration and enhance learning, yet its implementation occurs within a complex sociolinguistic landscape where practical classroom realities are often overlooked. This study investigates the on-the-ground enactment of the trilingual policy in primary classrooms. Its objectives are to analyse the pedagogical strategies teachers employ, identify the tensions that arise during implementation, and understand the factors that constrain or facilitate effective multilingual teaching. A qualitative, multi-site case study was conducted across six primary schools in two regions. Data were generated through no

proposer: Aminata Ngo Bisseck, Emmanuel Nwana Tumasang, Chantal Mforteh Mbuh

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The paper describes an empirical, qualitative study of policy implementation in real classrooms (2026 date suggests future/preprint status). Implementation realities are contingent on local context, teacher agency, and student demographics—findings are provisionally valid but not universally generalizable. The policy itself exists (TRUE) but its effectiveness is context-dependent and under ongoing evaluation.
